All right, so who's pumped? Is that good? OK, so we are in the home stretch of this summit. So up to this point, who fears right now we could just end and you know exactly what you need to do when you go home? Score. So all of you guys, when you come here, you feel inspired by one another. And I have to say just from our side, we appreciate so much how vehemently you guys defend us. I know that the bigger we get, the more likely we are to piss someone off. And so the noise continues to grow. Honestly, I'm actually really disconnected from it. I hear about it. I don't actually see it. But I know that a lot of you guys in individual circumstances and comments and messages that you guys, you reach out to people who clearly have misunderstandings and you guys basically let them know your truth, which I just want to say I appreciate you guys defending us and yourselves on our behalf. So thank you for that. So what I wanted to talk about today, or at least in this talk, is the concept of weaponized community. And so I think in this speaking slot, last summit, I want to say last year, because it feels like a year ago, but last summit, talked about weaponized compassion. Everyone remember that one? OK. And I know that was one of the favorite talks of last summit. And so this is that on steroids in a really cool way. And so my goal here is to show you how to do it for yourself. Sweet? All right. So quick acknowledgments. I am not the one who has come up with all the concepts that I'm about to explain to you. But these are the three primary influences that I had that have influenced how we approach, how we created this brand. So Perry Bertrand's secret selling system, Dan Kennedy's personality and copy, and Expert Secrets by Russell Brunson. Just as a side note, if you guys do have the opportunity to read Dan Kennedy's personality copy, it's 29 pages. Like it's nothing. I remember the first time I read it. I read it, and I literally started right back at the beginning, and then I read it again, because it was that good. So that being said, let's dig in. Why would you ever want to build a weaponized community? One, a weaponized community creates sticky customers. So higher lifetime value. Two, they allow price premiums. And the higher your price premiums, the higher likely that you are generating more and more profit. Three, it will massively increase referrals and virality. What I mean by that from the referral side is that you guys have been a bigger lead generation source than Facebook for us. And so as Mike was saying earlier, you are the generating source of the hottest leads that we have. And OK, here's a good one. Please do actually raise your hand. And a lot of people are like, hey, can you raise your hand if? Who here came to this community at the recommendation of someone else? Look, how important do you think Facebook is? Very. How do you think that is more? You saw how many hands went up. That's the game. That's the game. So if you can do that at your gym and raise your hand and ask that question, who here came from ads? Who here came from other people? That's when you get the last part, which is the virality. And so what also happens from an advertising standpoint is that your cost per acquisition goes down because of the positive engagement that we get on every ad, which I super thank you guys for. Whenever you guys see our ads, you're always like, I love this one. Or like, this guy's legit. Like, he helped me out. Like, you should trust him. And like, those little things are the little things that get people to get their snap judgment. And then they see something else. And they're like, I'll go for it. They're on the toilet. Their thumb just twitches a little bit faster, right? Because if you're caught, I'm half joking. Not really that joking. But you get my point. And so like, having a weaponized community allows you to do that. Four, it increases your business's robustness. So there's a book called Anti-Fragility or Anti-Fragile. Oops. You guys probably have heard of it. But basically, there are some businesses that break when times are in turmoil, when there's harder times. And there are some businesses that thrive, right? That actually do better than they would normally do. And so the goal is to make your business as robust or anti-fragile as possible so that while everyone else, kind of like Mike was saying, the people who know what they're doing love the economic downturns, because then we take everything, right? And I want like, right now, if in your mind, you're not like, I'm going to take everything. Then you need to get to that point as fast as possible, because it is coming. Fifth, it will make you more money. Cool. Everyone wants that. Great. So Apple, obviously they have a weaponized community. People get tattoos about it. Harley-Davidson, people wait years to get a motorcycle. And so they're, oh, and hopefully, yeah, that one you might recognize. Woo. So what I'm going to do is tell you the components of a weaponized community and how to use them and, I guess, weaponize your community. Now I was going to try and think of some cool way to say that. But that's what it is. So if you're a charismatic leader, one, two, you have a future-based cause. Three, you have unique vocabulary. Four, you have an indoctrination process. Five, you have uniform. Six, you have a new identity. So these are the components that create a weaponized community. I was really hesitant to show you what I'm about to show you. But I will, but you'll understand why. So all great companies and cultures are based on stories. All great companies and cultures are based on stories. Stories make people enter a different cognitive state. People are literally more suggestible. And like, which is a, like, being more suggestible is a double-edged sword. You can either use it to help someone or hurt someone. But you have more influence over that person's beliefs. We try to sell stories so that we can hopefully break the beliefs that you have that are no longer serving you and replace them with beliefs that will. That's the objective. And you should do the same thing with your customers. So think about it from that angle. Knowing how to tell stories is one of the most powerful skills that you can learn because it gives you influence. So, and because we can't obviously accomplish anything on our own, once you go from solopreneur to your first couple of employees, you need influence. You need to get them to do what you want them to do. And people think of that as control, but it's much more about leading and alignment than it is about controlling someone. It's like, I want to align everything that you believe with where we're going. And so we're all kind of on our own path, but we're going the same way. And then that's what creates these movements. So there was this movie. Anyone seen this? It's on Amazon. It's not considered a great movie. Anyways, it was like one of those days. It was Saturday. Laila and I were like, all right, we got two and a half hours. And Alex's brain is dead. So I was like, let's watch this movie because I like sci-fi stuff. I like superpower stuff. It's just my dig. So anyways, this movie's premise was there was some plague. Wipes out all the kids. Only like 5% of the kids survive. And they, once they survive, get one of four powers. And they name them by color. So the green kids get smart. The blue kids, which is the white dude, can control things like telekinetic. The yellow girl can control electricity. So there's like lots of yellows, lots of blues. And there's only two oranges left because the government targeted all the oranges to get rid of them. Why? Because they had the most powerful skill of all, which is they could control other people. And that's why they got rid of them. Because if you can influence your employees and then you can influence your customers, you can make massive changes far greater than what you can do, which is why it is the most valuable skill that you will need to learn. So my goal is to show you how to use stories to do that. So I want to show you how we've implemented all of these tactics I'm about to share with you in creating gym boards. And now you can do the same thing at your gyms. So first, in order to know how to layer stories together, you need to know how to actually tell a story. So anyone know someone who sucks at telling jokes? Like it's kind of funny that Mike said this. Anyone know someone who sucks at telling jokes? You're like, please don't tell the joke. You're going to ruin it. Like, don't tell the story. You're going to butcher it. You say that. And they're like, you just had to be there. It's because you suck at telling stories. And it means that they don't have the skill set to take you there so that you can feel what was felt. That's what makes stories compelling. That's what gets us to enter a different cognitive state that makes us more suggestible, makes us more able to learn. So mind you, this is in our biology. The reason stories are so compelling for us as humans is because for a very long time, it was the only way of transmitting information. And so the only way we could learn, hey, don't touch fire, is someone telling a story about it. And then you didn't have to make the same mistake. So it's a huge competitive advantage for survival for us to be able to understand how stories work. So first component of a story is a backstory. Every great story starts with a backstory. You're beginning with Spider-Man. He's like, I was just a normal kid. You hear it. It shows him in his neighborhood. Before anything happens, you have to build the character. And this includes, and the things that make it relevant are not the person, but the internal, external desires. So the external desires that person wants to accomplish externally that everyone else could see, and then internally, why? And I'll give examples of this. The second is the problem with their current way of life. Something like, it doesn't have to be a way of life, but some problem arises that is what the story is about. There is this person, XYZ, and this happened. Then after there's the problem, there's an epiphany. There's new information. There's a breaking point. Something else comes into the picture that gives them a path. They start walking down that path, and then they hit a conflict. After the conflict, they overcome, and then they have a transformation at the end, which is usually some form of external accomplishment with an internal transformation. Those are the components of a story. For those of you who did an interview with us, some of you guys, do you want to interview? OK. Do you know what the questions were? I was taking you down a story. And so the reason that our testimony videos convert way better than any of our competitors, actually, I don't even think that you use testimony videos. I'm just kidding. She's kidding. I wish everyone the best. Is because you have to capture the emotion, and the only way to do that is like this. It's following the sequence. And so if you want to get your clients to tell testimonials that are compelling, that get people to feel the way that person felt, you need to teach them how to tell the story. And so you don't have to teach them the skill, but you ask the questions the right way. So the Ham and the Garlic, the Cumin and the, whatever, whatever Dave said. Yeah, the Cumin and the Salami. So the Ham and the Garlic. So backstory. And you guys have heard me tell stories super quick, because you can convey information more quickly that way. So backstory. Boy loves his dog, wants his dog to be happy. If I really wanted to make it better, I'd be like, Joe. Joe has his dog, Joey has his dog, wants to make his dog Alfred happy. But he also wants to be a responsible kid to make his dad proud. His dad's not home a lot, but he wants to make his dad proud. So his dad bought him the dog. So it's a really important thing to him. But the dog almost didn't have ticks. Joey's really sad because he's like, oh my god, I love my dog, but my dad's going to come home. He's going to think I'm not taking care of him. He's going to think I'm a bad son. Right. So he's like, I have to figure out a way to solve this. So he goes on his little quest, talks to his grandmother, and finally she has the answer, which is you need to give the god garlic. So he sweats out the garlic. He eats it and sweats it out. The ticks will go away. He's like, OK, I'll do it. Tastes the garlic. Goes up the mountain to his dog. I'm kidding. And gives the dog the garlic. But then the dog doesn't eat the garlic. Joey's heartbroken. He's like, what am I going to do? How am I going to get back? He's going to see this. He's going to think I'm a failure. My dog hates me. My life is over. Right. Then he realizes, because he goes back to his grandmother, the sage, the janitor in the movie, who's just mopping, telling the character the things that they need to do with their life. He's like, hey, maybe you should try wrapping it in ham. Maybe the dog leeded that way. And he's like, epiphany. And then he goes. He wraps it in garlic, gives it to his dog. The dog's with the garlic. Ticks go up. And he has a transformation. Internally, now he's a responsible kid who makes his dad proud for taking care of things. And the achievement is the dog is tick-free. It's a very simple story. You guys have heard it before. And I figured this would give you at least an example of what makes it. If I wanted you to become more emotionally involved with the character, I'd talk far more about his internal desires. Why was this important to him? Why does it matter? What does it tell him about himself? And a lot of those whys tend to come back to family and childhoods, because there are a lot of things that really are the formative years for us. So with that said, let's look at how you can use simple stories like this to weaponize community. You don't need new. You need to get better at the fundamentals. Daily listening to sales training, two to three times close rate, concept of a product, cost of time to get value of benefit. How good you are and how much you get paid are unrelated. Told Mike about that. I strongly disagree with Joe Polis's quote. I think how good you are and how much you get paid are very strongly related. Most people actually aren't that good. I think they are. Three, value of discrepancy is the secret to retention in business. You always get paid for the thing you sold first, second. So we give away a challenge on the front end. We get paid on the membership based on how well we did on the front end. How well we do in launch indicates how much we get paid in legacy. So if we're fulfilling an amazing job in launch, more people will stay in legacy on the back end. I won't get into each one of them because you should already know a lot of these. Having a launch model for recurring revenue, always creating new stuff to keep people engaged. Confront the whole price up front whenever possible. Price anchor. Payment options in terms of half the battle. High ticket acquisition. Downsail your upsell. The closure formula, belief breaking formula. Chase time, not money. People need to be sold to be served. No one values what they don't pay for. The more they pay, the more they value it. My single mom free challenge story. Pain is the pitch. If they cry, they buy. Client finance acquisition. Sell the vacation out to plane flight. Speed is king. Speed is king for imitation. Speed is how you win. Agree on price, then agree on terms. The number of retouches directly correlated with the number of sales made. A sale is always made whether you sell someone on the conviction or they sell you on their excuse broke. People are easier to sell because they part with money more easily. Rich people use to sell because they have lots of money, so it's easier for them to part with a little bit of it. And so all of these things probably resonate with you because I say them over and over and over and over again. Because they actually are what I truly believe. I don't need to look at this list to know what I believe. But making the list reminded me of my beliefs. And then with each of those things, you can even see a couple of notes of an associated story that went with it. Third part of charismatic leader is there has to be something that makes them exceptional. So I have the mustache. I didn't have a mustache when I started this business. Did you guys notice that? Why did I grow a mustache? I grew a mustache for a couple of reasons. One, because I was actually nervous before the first. Before the first summit. And it reminded me to not take myself too seriously. Second reason is because people on a subconscious level like people more that they have recognizable facial features. Third, no one else had one. And I thought it was ridiculous. And I thought I could put a brand on it. And I think I have. So some of you guys have heard this. Harvard GMAT score, 4,101 closes, 6 gyms by 27, all of cash flow, open every facility at full capacity, 0 to 10 million to 10 months. My friend Jeff Bezos, 30 cities in 18 months. I found my wife with a funnel, married in eight days, came back from zero, didn't file bankruptcy. I even have my bank screenshots. Never had a business plan. Turned down whatever money things. Done, who's the cookie guy? Who's actually here. You guys have heard me tell the story about the trainer who stopped being a trainer to open a cookie store, because he was passionate about being good at business, not necessarily helping people lose weight. He's actually here. And we brought 200 people into his store off of a campaign that took 85 minutes. Top secret clearance, blah, blah, blah, blah. These are the exceptional things. These are the enemies. Marketing agencies, fake gurus, waste, revenue or profit, crappy customer service, work-life balance, complaining, excuses, ego, showing off, scarcity of mindset, copy competition, limiting beliefs, experts, especially certified experts, opportunity cost, sunk cost, fear of failure, perfection, attention drains, lack of excellence, flawed. I have seven failed partnerships. I almost went bankrupt. DUI and a head-on collision. Used to drink a half a bottle of black label every night. Great memory from knowledge and names, terrible for events and what I'm supposed to do. Disorganized, typically not as good with details. Try not to speak that over myself, but you get the point. Obsessive, forget to eat, forget to sleep. I lost 22 pounds when Layla went away for four weeks. Seriously, she didn't feed me. I was like, okay, I guess I'm gonna keep working. It's a big story. So these are belief breaking stories that I have, sell the vacation out the plane flight, Marbles in attention, Ham in the Garlic, business like, Garden Outta Machine, right? The four minute mile, my guys outsold everyone else because they didn't know any better. I had trainers who were selling 100% of people on some of them packages, because I didn't tell them it was a sale. I was like, so what you're gonna do is you're gonna tell them what they're gonna take, and they're gonna sign here, and they're gonna put their credit card down. I didn't tell them it was a sale. And they were really young dudes, and they were like, okay, that's what happens after you weigh them in. They just, they circled the things, and then they put their card down. They didn't know, so they didn't have any emotion about it, so they would go in and close everyone, and they didn't even know they were closing people. They just thought that was the part of the site. I was like, they weigh in, they do this, they do this, and then they fill out the paper. Like, that was what they knew, right? That's a story for those of you who get freaked out about sales. Sometimes you do better if you don't even think about it as a sale, right? This is what they're supposed to do. And so, the mechanic overpriced, all these things, right? The unicorn clothes, holiday overcome clothes, guarantees never lose, selling all these things. Chocolate and vanilla, they assume clothes on flavors. All these things, all these epiphany stories. I had it on an Excel sheet two years ago. So, you're like, great, Alex, I'm glad you could build Jim Warts. Write down your stories. The stories literally are what make who you are. So, some of you guys are Tony Robbins fans. We are really just a compendium. I don't know if that's the right word, but it sounds good. A compendium of the stories that we tell ourselves, right? The stories that we either choose to play again and again in our mind that we want to reinforce, or the ones that we choose not to think about, right? But we are just the stories that make us, and we want to explain someone who's new to someone else. First thing we do is tell a story, we give context. And so the question is, are you giving context to your community about who you are and what you believe in? And so you have to tell these stories after you write them down to your team and to your clients. It is equally important, if not more important, to tell your team, because your team will tell your clients the stories that you tell them, right? And that's part of what happens when you become sort of like a mythical figure, I'm not saying I am, but I'm saying like the point is, you're trying to create, like all of that stuff is from how they create comic book heroes. They have the origin story, they have the moment, Uncle Ben dies, like all of these things are crafted to create things that are compelling, right? So you're gonna tell them those stories over and over and over and over again, and then I mention repeat them. Why? Because people take multiple times to hear something. Some of you guys are probably on your phone right now, or you're thinking about your kid, or you're thinking about your wife, or the romance thing that we had yesterday, like what am I gonna say to my wife when I get back? What am I gonna say to my husband when I get back? Because you're not here right now, and that's okay. But somebody else is here, and they are paying attention to what I'm saying, and so the next time I hear it, maybe I'll get the husband person, and then you might not be paying attention, right? People watch reruns. So it was one of the biggest things that I had overcome was realizing that I had this belief that if I said something once, I should never say it again. I was like, I don't wanna repeat myself, you know what I mean? Because I don't wanna sound like I'm a broken record, right? People like to be reminded more than they like to be taught, but they need to be reminded more than they need to be taught. It's probably both. But people watch reruns. They know how it's going to end, and they watch it still, because that's how much we like stories. And so the question is, can you deliver your beliefs in the package of a story that still continues to keep people's attention? Does that make sense? So people watch Grant Cardone every day, right? That's some of you do. Uncle G, he only has three messages. You can't learn this in school, man. I can teach you this in school, right? Number one, two, you gotta sell. Sell or be sold, right? Number two, three, gotta put your money in real estate. It's the only way to get rich, right? That's it. Three messages, it's all he's got. He just rotates, nothing else. And we watch because we know that we still haven't learned it yet. So we watch it again, and again, and again, and again. Which is why the more general, like, you've got the Gary Vee's, not that he's got his hustle and grind mantra, I'm not saying this is only stuff, but the most widely consumed stuff is that. Because on some level, the broadest market is seekers. People who haven't started, y'all are not seekers. You already have businesses. But the largest market is people who don't have businesses and are dissatisfied with their lives. And so what do those people wanna watch every day? I really should do something. Tomorrow, I really should do something. I'm gonna watch it again, I really should do something. And it's a hundred times, and then finally, it clicks. And so people need to be reminded more than they need to be taught. So, quick recap for just the leader section. I know this is kind of deep. A lot of stuff here. Leader stories, these are the division categories. You need to know what your evolution, your origins, what made you who you are, what are the stories that created the character that is you. When you're looking at those things, you wanna look at your beliefs. What are the things that formed your beliefs? I used to think that broke people were broke. And then I had this thought one day. I was like, what if broke people aren't good with money? I was like, that's why they're broke, because they spend it all. I was like, which means they should spend it with me. And that was my epiphany. And that's one of the stories that I tell myself that's created the beliefs that I have. What are the things that make you exceptional? The thing is that you can't have exceptional things without flaws. If Superman didn't have kryptonite, he wouldn't have a comic book, because he'd be unbeatable. There'd be nothing, there'd be no story there. There's no drama, he'd just never lose. Enemies, you have to have enemies. You have to have people you're throwing rocks at. What are you against? Saying what you're against is almost more powerful than what you're saying you're for. And so you have to be purposely polar in some instances. And so in the example that I gave, I'm very polar when it comes to marketing agencies and gurus and not doing it yourself. You need to learn, right? You have to learn. And so I speak out very strongly about that. You have to have the flaws, like I said. And then the language gives you in-group, out-group dynamic. So it speeds up communication, makes communication more efficient, which increases affinity. The more you communicate with someone, the more efficiently you communicate with someone, the more you like them. Who do you text back the fastest? Probably the person you sleep with, right? Epiphany stories. These should be the cornerstone stories of what make your you-you. But I delineate this as like, this is what makes me me kind of the top two. And then this is like, what has shifted how I view my subject matter? So think about that in terms of like, what shaped your views on fitness, on helping people lose weight on transforming lives? Like what are those stories? Okay. And you have to write down what you truly believe, not what you think you should believe. Because people want to know the difference. Because if you don't truly believe it, you won't talk about it. Because you don't actually believe it. And especially what people will disagree with you on. So like, if you're like, I want to transform lots of lives, I believe that, cool, so does everyone. Like that's not exceptional. Helping mankind is not a belief that is valuable from a storytelling standpoint. It's just not. Being against work-life balances, like you should be able to tell something a story or a belief and get some people to not want to work for you. So when we say, work-life balances isn't a thing at Gym Watch. We believe in work-life integration. Work all the time. But you live all the time. We just call it life. That's a belief we have. If you want to be off at five, this is not the place for you to work. And that's okay. You should go find something like that. It's not here. So you have to have these beliefs that should turn people away. If they don't, then they are not useful in creating a tribe that is specific for you. So you have to have the courage to be you, actually. And so you'll attract the people who are like you. You guys are all, in a lot of ways, I think, I think this community is a lot like each other. People are like, man, I just love the vibe here. Like everyone's so giving, everyone's so positive. Who here has been to any other type of fitness business, whatever? Anyone? The vibe is the vibe different. The vibe is different. The vibe is predicated on the stories that we have told to reinforce the beliefs about what we are about, about what we believe in. And that vibe is real value. That vibe from an organizational standpoint is the culture, which is what retains people, which is everything that Layla talked about yesterday. But these stories are what influence people to believe. And obviously you want to repel the people who aren't like you. If you're like, man, this person's not a good fit. If your culture were strong enough, they would be repelled, right? They'd be like, it's not my vibe. And we're like, cool, we don't have to deal with it. So it's why actually having a uniform that is in alignment with your values is important at your gym. So the raggy sweats doesn't really say premium. So last piece is new identity, and this is big. So create a new aspirational identity. Ideally, it should be an I am statement. I am a gym lord, right? So why is it so important? Because with it comes a new set of values, even more so than anything else that I covered. This is where new identities can either be help or manipulation, right? They can either help people or hurt people, they can liberate or they can suppress people, they can teach or they can enslave, right? That's what our identity does. The stories we tell ourselves are our identity. We're comprised of those stories. And so when we help someone step into a new identity, an aspirational identity, it allows them to more easily shed old beliefs that no longer serve them and adopt new beliefs of the new identity. And so new identities help people adopt new beliefs faster. So a lot of you guys have had, had, don't anymore, but had really limiting beliefs around making money, right? Some people didn't like selling things, some people didn't like advertising, some people didn't want, didn't like, I hate all employees. Okay, that's not really gonna serve you very well, right? Are there some things on that list that you can't even say in your head without feeling something? Right? That's how powerful I am statements are. Some of them make you feel proud. Some of them make you feel disgusted. That is how much power I am has, which is why I always tell everyone to be careful about what you speak over yourself. I just, I am not that good at sales. And the reason that I showed it again and again is because depending on the people who are new, it was someone's first time. And for those of you who are not, it reinforces the story that reminds you of why I do what I do and why I believe what I believe and why you believe what you believe and why that's an alignment with you being here. So once you master this, people will begin to come to you and you will increase demand for you.
